title: Blinded by Sight: Seeing Race through the Eyes of the Blind By Osagie K. Obasogie Stanford University Press. 2013. 288 pages. $24.95 paper, $85.00 cloth
abstract: This book explores how blind people make sense of race in the United States. Obasogie uses the case of blind people to demonstrate that race is a powerful social construct that persists even for those who are unable to see skin color. Osagie K. Obasogie draws upon in-depth qualitative interviews with 106 blind respondents and 25 sighted respondents. While the blind respondents have been blind their entire lives, the data show that they often develop a strong understanding of racial boundaries early in life. Experiences such as making friends and developing romantic relationships teach blind people about the salience of racial boundaries. In addition, Obasogie finds that the blind may rely on other senses such as smell and touch to develop an understanding of race. As a result, blind respondents in this study are highly conscious of race and can serve as active agents in the reproduction of racial boundaries in the United States.
